game_name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles IV: Turtles in Time

danger_signals.meaning: Active Foot Clan threat in current zone; danger present

danger_signals.meaning: Intensified enemy opposition; historical era threats manifesting

danger_signals.meaning: Critical danger moment; major world-state change imminent

danger_signals.visual_form: Transition between historical era levels (past to future)
danger_signals.meaning: Player displaced; world-state fundamentally altered; navigation challenge escalating

danger_signals.visual_form: Airwave takeover with Shredder laughing (narrative event)
danger_signals.meaning: Foot Clan operational control of communication; crisis escalation signal

safety_signals.meaning: Civilian ally; information source; connection to normal world order

safety_signals.visual_form: Four playable characters present (Leonardo, Michelangelo, Donatello, Raphael)
safety_signals.meaning: Full team operational; defensive capability optimal

safety_signals.meaning: Civilization stable; national symbol secure; world-state normalized

safety_signals.visual_form: Urban civilian environment with Foot activity containable
safety_signals.meaning: Threat manageable in familiar home territory; tactical advantage

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Foot symbol (implicit in faction name); Shredder's iconic helmet/armor
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Foot Soldiers (standard), Stone Warriors (variant/enhanced), Tokka and Rahzar (specialized lieutenants), Bebop & Rocksteady (comedic heavy units)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Technodrome (main base); presence across all time periods indicating temporal organization reach

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Turtle mask colors; individual weapon styles
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: NYC sewers (home base); news media connection through April O'Neil

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Statue of Liberty; news media infrastructure (April O'Neil)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Downtown NYC streets; Liberty Island; television broadcast infrastructure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Super Krang steals Statue of Liberty; Shredder hijacks broadcast media
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turtles mobilize from home; player gains control and begins combat across levels

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Foot Soldiers and themed enemy types fill each level; multiple boss encounters (Baxter Stockman, Metalhead, Cement Man, etc.)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies attack in waves; bosses execute scripted attack patterns; players must defeat progressively stronger opponents

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level transitions between historical eras (streets → sewers → Technodrome → past/future zones); visual style shifts per era
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turtles thrown through time by Shredder; must navigate multiple temporal environments; enemy types may reflect historical setting

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Technodrome level features advanced Foot technology; Super Krang and Krang in UFO encountered
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Escalated boss difficulty; final tier opposition; narrative culmination point

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shredder defeated; Statue of Liberty returned to place
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game completion; return to normal world order; Foot Clan threat neutralized

environmental_storytelling.example: Movement from NYC streets → sewers → Technodrome reflects deepening into enemy territory and escalating threat severity
Historical era representations (past/future levels) communicate that Shredder's influence spans temporal boundaries; Foot Clan operates across time

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss character design as faction authority
environmental_storytelling.example: Encountering named bosses (Baxter Stockman, Metalhead, Super Krang) signals Foot Clan hierarchy and strategic importance; boss defeat removes specific tactical assets
Foot Soldiers as baseline threat; Stone Warriors as regional variant; specialized lieutenants (Tokka, Rahzar, Bebop, Rocksteady) signal faction organization and resource commitment
April O'Neil reporting civilian perspective; Statue of Liberty theft establishes scale and severity; Shredder's broadcast takeover shows propaganda/control attempt

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Attack in groups; slam-able into other enemies; indicate ongoing Foot presence
Tougher variant; indicate escalating enemy quality within level or historical era
Executes scripted attack pattern; defeat removes specific threat; Japanese version includes post-defeat dialogue ('good for nothing turtles')
Robotic opponent; scripted combat sequence; Japanese version defeat dialogue ('I've been crushed')
Only boss with dialogue in English versions; UFO phase indicates final confrontation territory
Orchestrates crisis (statue theft, broadcast hijack, time warp); final boss; defeat resolves world-state crisis
Appears in opening cutscene reporting; establishes normal world baseline; framing device for crisis introduction
Player-controlled combat; respond to input; can be selected in 2-player mode or assigned in 4-player mode; each has individual strengths/weaknesses

notes: Sources heavily focused on regional/developmental differences rather than visual manifestation documentation. World-state communication inferred from plot description, level progression, and boss encounters. Game uses classical beat-em-up visual grammar: level progression = narrative advancement, boss encounters = plot beats, enemy density/type = threat escalation. Color-coding via turtle masks is primary faction identification in 4-player context. Time travel mechanic communicates that Foot Clan threat transcends normal spatial/temporal boundaries. Japanese version includes boss post-defeat dialogue absent from English releases, suggesting enhanced narrative characterization in original design.
sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (regional differences, boss dialogue)
